Member-only story

JavaScript scope is not confusing because the rules are difficult. It is confusing because your mental model is wrong — and AI often reinforces the wrong one.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Every JavaScript developer eventually reaches a point where the language starts behaving like it has a personal grudge.
A variable exists.
Then it doesn’t.
A closure remembers something you expected it to forget.
A callback logs 5 five times.
A seemingly harmless refactor breaks an application that has been working for months.
The common explanation is that JavaScript scope is “weird.”
It isn’t.
It is remarkably consistent.
The problem is that most junior developers never build the mental model required to predict what the engine is actually doing. They memorize var, let, and const. They learn lexical scope as a definition. They can explain hoisting in an interview.
Then production happens.
And suddenly none of those definitions help.
